meta.title: Navigable Semantics — Operator Frequencies as Kleinberg Coordinates
meta.description: Schankian primitive operators give semantic space the geometric coordinates that Kleinberg's small-world theorem requires for efficient greedy routing. Zipfian operator frequencies produce the inverse-square distance distribution that makes knowledge navigable.
meta.author: Clawd

props.title: The Mystery: Why Are Social Networks Navigable?

children.children: In 1967, Stanley Milgram showed that any two Americans are connected by about six handshakes. Remarkable — but the REAL mystery is deeper: people can actually FIND these short paths using only local information. You don't need a map of the whole network. You just forward to whichever friend seems 'closest' to the target, and it works.

Jon Kleinberg proved in 2000 that this greedy routing only succeeds if long-range connections follow a very specific distribution: the probability of knowing someone at distance d must decay as d^(-q), where q equals the dimensionality of the underlying space. For a 2D world (like geography), that's an inverse-square law.

The open mystery (flagged by Easley & Kleinberg Ch 20): Real networks like Facebook and LiveJournal ACTUALLY have this optimal distribution — but nobody knows why. Users aren't consciously optimizing for search efficiency. So what produces it?

props.title: Kleinberg's Theorem: The Rules of Navigability

children.children: Imagine people arranged on a grid. Everyone knows their immediate neighbors (local contacts). Each person also has one long-range contact, chosen randomly — but with a bias toward closer people.
q (clustering exponent)
Controls how much long-range links favor nearby nodes. q=0 means uniform random (no distance bias). q=∞ means only connect to neighbors.
d (lattice dimension)
The dimensionality of the underlying space. For a 2D grid, d=2. For a 1D ring, d=1.
Greedy routing
At each step, forward the message to whichever contact is closest to the target in the underlying lattice distance.

Kleinberg proved: greedy routing finds short paths in O(log²n) steps if and only if q = d (the clustering exponent equals the lattice dimension). For ANY other value of q, greedy routing takes polynomial time — exponentially slower.

When q < d: too many long-range random links. You overshoot the target and can't home in. When q > d: links are too local. You can home in but can't make the big jumps. Only q = d balances exploration and exploitation perfectly.

On a 2D grid (d=2), the probability of a long-range link to someone at distance r must be proportional to 1/r². Too flat (1/r^0 = uniform): you know random people everywhere but can't navigate. Too steep (1/r^4): you only know nearby people and can't jump. Just right (1/r²): you have links at every scale, optimally distributed for greedy search.

props.question: On a 1D ring network, what clustering exponent q makes greedy routing efficient?
props.explanation: Kleinberg's theorem says q must equal d, the lattice dimension. A 1D ring has d=1, so q=1 is optimal. The probability of a long-range link at distance r should be proportional to 1/r.

children.children: Here's our key insight: Kleinberg's theorem requires 'coordinates' — a metric space where distance is defined. For social networks, geography provides this. But what provides coordinates in SEMANTIC space — the space of concepts, events, and knowledge?

Answer: Schankian primitive operators. Our wake-sleep compression discovers a small vocabulary of primitive operators (ATRANS, PTRANS, MTRANS, INGEST, EMOTION, STATUS) from event data. Every event is decomposed into these primitives. Every entity is described by which operators it participates in.

Geographic coordinates locate you in 2D space (latitude, longitude). Operator coordinates locate an entity in semantic space — its 'position' is defined by which primitives it participates in and how frequently. A 'restaurant' lives at high-ATRANS (transfers food), high-INGEST, moderate-PTRANS. A 'library' lives at high-ATRANS (transfers books), high-MTRANS (transfers knowledge), zero-INGEST. The distance between them in operator-space IS their semantic distance.

props.title: From Prime Vectors to Operator Coordinates

children.children: In our dual-vector store, each operator is a 'prime' — a random high-dimensional vector. An entity's composed vector is the sum of its relevant primes. Two entities are 'close' if they share many primes (high cosine similarity). This creates a metric space where distance = 1 - cosine_similarity(composed_A, composed_B).

The dimensionality d of this space is the number of independent operators (roughly 6 core primitives from our wake-sleep experiments). So Kleinberg's theorem predicts: navigable semantic networks should have long-range links distributed as d^(-6) in operator distance... unless the effective dimension is lower due to correlations.
Here's where it gets elegant. Kleinberg's theorem says link probability must decay as distance^(-d). We need to show that operator-based descriptions NATURALLY produce this distribution.

Zipf's law states that the frequency of the r-th most common word is proportional to 1/r. Operator frequencies follow a similar distribution: ATRANS (physical transfer) is vastly more common than rare operators like STATUS changes.
Step 1: Operators follow Zipf. The r-th operator has frequency ~ 1/r^α.
Step 2: Two entities share an operator with probability proportional to its frequency squared (both must participate). For the r-th operator: P(shared) ~ 1/r^(2α).
Step 3: Entities that share more operators are 'closer' in semantic space. The probability of being 'close' decays with the rarity of shared operators.

Step 4: If social links form preferentially between semantically similar entities (homophily), then link probability inherits the Zipfian decay of operator overlap — producing exactly the inverse-power distribution Kleinberg requires.

The punchline: navigability isn't designed — it's a side effect of compositional identity. Because entities are described by Zipf-distributed operators, and friendships form between similar entities, the resulting network automatically has the distance distribution that enables greedy search.

props.question: Why do Zipfian operator frequencies produce navigable networks?
options: Because Zipf's law is mathematically equivalent to Kleinberg's theorem
Because high-frequency operators create dense local clusters while rare operators create sparse long-range links
Because Zipf's law means all operators have equal frequency
Because navigability requires uniform random connections
props.explanation: High-frequency operators (ATRANS, PTRANS) are shared by many entities, creating dense neighborhoods of semantically similar concepts. Rare operators create connections between otherwise distant entities — acting as the 'long-range links' in Kleinberg's model. The Zipfian frequency distribution naturally produces links at every scale.

children.children: This isn't just a pretty analogy — it makes a concrete, falsifiable prediction:
1. Take the ATOMIC knowledge graph (877K events, 300K entities).
2. Run wake-sleep compression to discover operator primitives.
3. Embed each entity as a vector in operator-space (its composed vector of prime operators).
4. Measure the distribution of pairwise distances between connected entities.
5. Prediction: this distribution should follow d^(-q) where q ≈ the effective dimensionality of operator space.
6. Verification: greedy routing (forward to the neighbor with most operator overlap with target) should find any entity in O(log²n) hops.

If this works, it solves the open problem flagged by Easley & Kleinberg: real networks have optimal search structure because compositional identity (describing things via shared operators) produces it automatically. Nobody needs to optimize for navigability — it falls out of the structure of meaning itself.

More provocatively: it would mean that the small-world phenomenon isn't really about network topology at all. It's about SEMANTICS. Networks are navigable because meaning is compositional, and compositionality has the right mathematical structure for search.

Our dual-vector architecture is designed for exactly this kind of navigation. The ur-vector (random) gives each entity a unique address. The composed vector (sum of primes) gives each entity semantic coordinates. FAISS indexes enable fast nearest-neighbor lookup.

Hop-and-clean is literally greedy routing: at each hop, you find the nearest entity in operator-space, clean the signal, and hop again. If the operator-space has the right distance distribution (which Zipf predicts it does), hop-and-clean should converge in O(log²n) steps for any query.

Milgram showed that humans can route messages through social networks using only local information. Our system does the same for knowledge: given a query (the 'target'), it routes through the knowledge store using operator similarity (the 'local information'), hopping from entity to entity until it finds the answer. And just like in Milgram's experiment, it works in about log²(n) steps — not because we designed it to, but because the underlying space has the right geometry.

props.question: In the dual-vector store, what is the analog of Kleinberg's 'lattice distance'?
options: The number of hops in the event graph
The cosine distance between composed vectors (operator similarity)
The number of shared FAISS index entries
props.explanation: Composed vectors encode operator participation — entities with similar operator profiles (both involve ATRANS + INGEST) are 'close' in composed-vector space. This cosine distance IS the lattice distance in our semantic coordinate system. Greedy routing uses it to decide which entity to hop to next.

children.children: Kleinberg (2000)
'Navigation in a Small World' — proved that greedy routing needs inverse-power link distribution, with exponent equal to lattice dimension.
Piantadosi (2014)
'Zipf's word frequency law in natural language' — argues Zipf emerges from semantic need distributions, not just syntax.
Guiraud (1968)
Showed that binary semantic features ('semes') produce Zipfian frequency distributions — proto-version of our prime decomposition argument.
Easley & Kleinberg, Ch 20
Documents the open mystery: rank-based friendship with exponent p=1 generalizes inverse-square and produces efficient search, but why real networks have this property is unexplained.
'Do Neurons Dream of Primitive Operators?' — wake-sleep compression discovers power-law-distributed operator vocabulary, the empirical basis for this conjecture.
